purpose: Determines whether the macro environment supports or undermines AI capex continuation by grounding analysis in accounting identity rather than pattern-matching
components: Private investment (AI capex at ~$250B as the entire growth margin)
Government fiscal deficit ($1.8-2T as level floor)
Trade balance and foreign savings
Corporate savings and household savings
CEO capex decisions as the binding variable (4-5 decision-makers)
current_assessment: AI capex constitutes the entire growth margin of private investment. Fiscal deficit provides a floor but not growth. Macro regime is a call option on 4-5 capital allocation decisions with no diversification across decision-makers. A 25% capex cut implies $50-60B earnings impact with ±30-50% uncertainty.
limitations: Specific magnitude estimates carry ±30-50% uncertainty
Cannot predict CEO decision timing
DOGE fiscal consolidation as exogenous loop-breaker is unmodeled
Does not account for multiplier effects or second-round transmission timing

purpose: Provides an invariant ordering framework for cycle positioning across technology investment booms, identifying which stage the current AI cycle occupies
components: Stage 1: Breadth deterioration (ACTIVE — confirmed with 800-1200bp equal-weighted underperformance)
Stage 2: Revenue validation failure (NOT YET TRIGGERED — no major hyperscaler AI revenue disappointment)
Stage 3: Capex rationalization (requires Stage 2 trigger)
Stage 4: Credit transmission (6-12 month lag from equity stress)
Stage 5: Macro contagion
current_assessment: Stage 1 is active and confirmed. Stage 2 has not triggered. The narrative validation window for Stage 2 is Q3 2026-Q2 2027 (±2-3 quarters). The self-funded capex structure may extend the patience period between stages beyond historical precedent. Stage boundaries are fuzzy but ordering is empirically strong across 7 cataloged episodes.
limitations: Stage boundaries are fuzzy and not precisely identifiable in real-time
Self-funded capex at 55% passive has no historical precedent
Specific thresholds (55-65% capex/OCF, 4 quarters ROIC < WACC) are refuted
Cannot distinguish Stage 1 duration from false positive

purpose: Provides a weighted historical comparison framework superior to single-analogue dependence for understanding the AI investment cycle
components: Internet/telecom 1996-2002 at 30% weight (capex structure, revenue validation dynamics)
Nifty Fifty 1968-1974 at 20% weight (concentration premium, institutional herding, career risk)
Conglomerate era 1960s-1980s at 15% weight (ROIC dilution despite revenue growth)
Shale 2010-2016 at 15% weight (self-funded rationalization, external price signal as catalyst)
Telecom at 10% weight (capex/revenue dynamics, but N=1 for threshold analysis)
Japan 1990s-2000s at 10% weight (slow-grind duration risk, 13-year underperformance template)
current_assessment: Composite approach favored over single primary analogue in pair_0 debate. Current cycle has features of each analogue but none dominates. The specific weights are debatable but the composite principle is well-supported. Single-analogue dependence exhibits the selective analogy deployment bias the KB itself identifies.
limitations: Specific weights are subjective and debatable
Each analogue has materially different macro backdrop
No analogue features ~55% passive indexation
Composite approach may underweight the truly novel aspects of the current cycle
Cross-analogue phase-position averaging produces noise (50-80% range spans nearly entire plausible range)

purpose: Detects regime mispricing by comparing implied growth, inflation, and disruption assumptions across equities, rates, credit, and currencies
components: Equity implied structural real growth (>2%)
Rates implied r* and growth (~1.5-1.8%)
Credit implied disruption probability (near zero)
Dollar implied capital flow sustainability
Winner-take-all equilibrium as fragile partial reconciliation
current_assessment: Roughly 60% genuine inconsistency across asset classes and 40% artifact of correlated estimation errors from shared input data. Winner-take-all equilibrium partially reconciles: equities right about Mag-7, rates right about broad economy, credit right about no systemic stress. The inconsistency is real but its magnitude as an independent signal is overstated.
limitations: Equity ERP, bond r*, and credit spreads share common input data, partially deflating their independent disagreement
Different market participants with different mandates process shared data through different loss functions
Model specification choices affect measured inconsistency
No formal framework to weight asset class signals against each other

purpose: Provides statistical discipline for confidence ratings by computing actual confidence intervals from available sample sizes, distinguishing analytical conviction from statistical reliability
components: Sample size assessment for each major claim
Binomial and bootstrap confidence interval computation
Power analysis against gradual vs sudden structural shifts
Separation of descriptive confidence (observable) from predictive confidence (forecasted)
Systematic comparison of stated confidence vs statistically defensible confidence
current_assessment: Most KB confidence ratings are inflated relative to statistical reliability. GPT diffusion lag (N=5-6) has 95% CI spanning ~53pp. Value capture (N=6) spans ~49pp. Equity-credit sequencing (N=3-6) spans 55-72pp. The research process needs to formally distinguish analytical conviction from statistical reliability in every confidence rating. TFP structural break tests have only 20-40% power against gradual shifts.
limitations: Cannot replace domain expertise and analytical judgment
Some claims are grounded in accounting identities or physics, not sample statistics
Low power against gradual shifts means absence of evidence is not evidence of absence
Does not address systematic biases in analogue selection
